instruction: Explain encapsulation to a beginner with a code example and one common mistake.
input: The learner knows classes and objects but confuses private fields with public fields.
output: Encapsulation means an object keeps its state inside the class and exposes safe operations for changing that state. In a BankAccount class, balance should be private so outside code cannot set it to an invalid value directly. Public methods like deposit validate the amount before changing the balance. A common mistake is to make every field public, which removes the class's ability to protect its own rules. Checkpoint: why should deposit reject a negative amount?

diagram.description: BankAccount has private balance and public deposit method

review.notes: Format example, not a full pilot record.

instruction: Walk through stack push and pop operations with Big-O complexity.
input: The learner has seen arrays but not LIFO behavior.
output: A stack follows LIFO: last in, first out. If we push 10, then 20, then 30, the top is 30. A pop removes 30 first, leaving 20 on top. Push, pop, and peek are O(1) when the top is tracked because each operation touches only one end. A common mistake is to remove from the bottom, which turns the structure into something closer to a queue and may require shifting elements.

diagram.description: Vertical stack with 30 on top of 20 on top of 10

review.notes: Format example, not a full pilot record.

instruction: Explain why binary search is O(log n).
input: The learner knows linear search and sorted arrays.
output: Binary search is O(log n) because each comparison removes about half of the remaining search space. If an array has 64 items, one comparison leaves about 32, then 16, then 8, and so on. The number of times you can halve n before reaching 1 is log2(n). The key condition is that the array must be sorted; without sorted order, discarding half the array would not be safe.

diagram.description: Search space halves from n to n/2 to n/4 to 1

review.notes: Format example, not a full pilot record.

instruction: Generate one multiple-choice question about linked-list insertion.
input: Focus on inserting a node at the front of a singly linked list.
output: {"question":"When inserting a new node at the front of a singly linked list, what should newNode.next point to before updating head?","options":["The old head","null","The last node","The new node itself"],"answer":"The old head","explanation":"The new node must link to the previous first node. Then head can move to the new node without losing the rest of the list."}

diagram.description: newNode points to old head, then head points to newNode

review.notes: Format example, not a full pilot record.

instruction: Respond Socratically to a learner who thinks polymorphism means any method with the same name.
input: Student answer: Polymorphism is when two methods have the same name.
output: You are close, but that answer mixes two related ideas. Same method name can describe overloading, where parameter lists differ, or overriding, where a subclass provides its own version of an inherited method. Runtime polymorphism usually means a superclass reference can call an overridden method on a subclass object, and Java chooses the subclass behavior at runtime. Quick check: if Shape s = new Circle(); calls s.draw(), whose draw method should run?

code.content:
Shape s = new Circle();
s.draw(); // Circle.draw runs if draw is overridden

diagram.description: Shape parent with Circle child overriding draw

review.notes: Format example, not a full pilot record.
